name: Port of Sa Creueta: loop from Olla de Altea
name_original: Puerto de Sa Creueta — Circular desde Olla de Altea
description:
Fertile and wild river plains, mountain paths, charming rural villages and a touch of the coast make this demanding route the perfect catalogue for a cycling adventure along the Costa Blanca. Along the more than 90 kilometres of the route, you pass through various towns with tourist services of all kinds. Even so, the mountain and cropped sections are more remote, so we recommend that you take everything you need to be self-sufficient.

The route is circular from the Olla Altea tram station. The path begins with a short climb along the mouth of the Algar River and then through Guadalest. A little later, you pass through Polop and enter demanding climbs along agricultural tracks and secondary roads until you cross Callosa d'en Sarrià and Tàrbena, on the way to the Sa Creueta mountain pass. Take it easy, as almost 2,000 metres of positive gradient await you in total.

From the port, a final steep climb awaits you, which ends by taking you down the Camí de la Llacuna, a friendly track that crosses the spectacular Alicante mountain landscape. Once you finish the descent, you roll along the plain of the Xaló River and enjoy its endless collage of orchards. This section passes through several towns that are ideal for making another stop and recharging your batteries.

Before returning to the coast, you pedal along the highway on tracks that take you back to the desert-like views and shades of red and ochre earth. Once on the coast, there are a couple of opportunities for a restorative dip in the sea.
region_intro: Gravel biking around Altea offers diverse landscapes, from rugged mountain ranges to scenic coastal paths. The region is characterized by the Sierra de Bernia, providing challenging climbs and panoramic views, alongside the fertile Guadalest Valley with its reservoir. Riders can expect a mix of winding roads through pine forests, citrus groves, and unpaved tracks leading to stunning viewpoints. This varied terrain makes Altea a prime destination for gravel cyclists seeking both demanding ascents and picturesque routes. Best gravel bike trails around Altea The most popular gravel bike trail is Port of Sa Creueta: loop from Olla de Altea, a 57.2 miles (92.1 km) trail that takes 8 hours 17 minutes to complete. This difficult route features significant elevation gain through mountainous terrain. Another top favourite among local gravel bikers is Sa Creueta Pass – Sa Creueta Pass (780 m) loop from Altea, a difficult 43.9 miles (70.6 km) path. This route includes the notable Sa Creueta Pass, offering expansive views of the surrounding landscape. Local gravel bikers also love the Blick on Guadalest – Guadalest Reservoir loop from Benidorm Intermodal, a 38.0 miles (61.2 km) trail leading through the scenic Guadalest Valley and past its reservoir, often completed in about 4 hours 17 minutes. Gravel biking around Altea is defined by rugged mountain ranges like the Sierra de Bernia, fertile valleys, and diverse coastal scenery. The network offers options for various ability levels, from moderate rides to challenging multi-hour adventures. The routes in Altea are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 3.6 stars from over 16 reviews. More than 400 gravel bikers have used komoot to explore Altea's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: What is the general difficulty level of gravel biking routes around Altea?
A: Gravel biking around Altea offers a wide range of difficulties. While there is 1 easy route, the majority of the 38 available routes are more challenging, with 10 moderate and 27 difficult options. Many routes feature significant elevation gains, making them suitable for riders with good to very good fitness levels. Some segments may even require pushing your bike.

Q: Are there any beginner-friendly gravel bike trails in Altea?
A: Yes, while many routes are challenging, there is 1 easy gravel bike trail available around Altea. These routes typically have minimal elevation and are suitable for shorter rides, often up to two hours. For those looking for a slightly more challenging but still manageable experience, there are also 10 moderate routes to explore.

Q: What kind of landscapes can I expect to see on gravel bike trails in Altea?
A: Altea's gravel bike trails traverse incredibly diverse landscapes. You'll ride through rugged mountain ranges like the Sierra de Bernia, offering challenging climbs and panoramic coastal views. Routes also wind through fertile valleys such as the Guadalest Valley, past citrus, olive, and almond groves, and along scenic coastal paths with cliffside views and picturesque bays. Expect a mix of winding roads, pine forests, and unpaved tracks.

Q: Are there any notable landmarks or viewpoints along the gravel bike routes?
A: Absolutely. Many routes offer breathtaking views and pass by significant landmarks. The Sierra de Bernia provides stunning vistas, and the Coll de Rates is legendary for its panoramic views extending to the coastline and even Ibiza on clear days. You might also encounter the historic Fort of Bernia or the natural tunnel of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/149038" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Forat de Bèrnia&lt;/a&gt;. The Albir Lighthouse path offers spectacular sea views, and Altea's Old Town itself provides numerous viewpoints over the Mediterranean.

Q: Can I find routes that include natural features like waterfalls or lakes?
A: Yes, the region is rich in natural beauty. The Algar River, originating in the Sierra de Bernia, is famous for its natural springs, crystal-clear waters, and picturesque waterfalls and pools, making for a refreshing stop. Additionally, you can find routes that pass by the Guadalest Reservoir, known for its stunning scenery. For specific highlights, consider exploring areas with features like &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4371964" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;El Saladar Saltworks&lt;/a&gt; or various viewpoints over the Salinas de Calpe.

Q: Are there any loop routes available for gravel biking in Altea?
A: Yes, many of the gravel biking routes around Altea are designed as loops, allowing you to start and end at the same point. A popular option is the challenging &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e1922610456/port-of-sa-creueta-loop-from-olla-de-altea" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Port of Sa Creueta: loop from Olla de Altea&lt;/a&gt;, covering 92.1 km. Another highly-rated loop is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/31038899" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sa Creueta Pass – Sa Creueta Pass (780 m) loop from Altea&lt;/a&gt;, which is 70.6 km long and offers expansive views.

Q: What is the best time of year for gravel biking in Altea?
A: Altea boasts a Mediterranean climate with over 300 sunny days a year, making it an excellent destination for year-round outdoor activities. The mild winters are particularly appealing for cycling, avoiding the intense heat of summer. Spring and autumn also offer pleasant temperatures and beautiful scenery, making them ideal for exploring the diverse terrain.

Q: What do other gravel bikers enjoy most about the trails in Altea?
A: The gravel biking routes around Altea are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 3.6 stars from over 16 reviews. Riders frequently praise the diverse landscapes, from rugged mountains to scenic coastal paths, and the challenging yet rewarding climbs. The variety of terrain, combining winding roads through forests and unpaved tracks, is also a common highlight.

Q: Are there any gravel routes that pass through the Guadalest Valley?
A: Yes, the Guadalest Valley and its reservoir are prominent features in many cycling routes around Altea. The valley is known for its stunning scenery and demanding inclines. A great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23744167" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Blick on Guadalest – Guadalest Reservoir loop from Benidorm Intermodal&lt;/a&gt;, a 61.2 km trail that takes you directly through this picturesque area.

Q: Are there any routes that include the famous Coll de Rates?
A: Yes, the legendary Coll de Rates, known for its challenging ascent and breathtaking views, is incorporated into some gravel biking routes. For an extensive ride that includes this iconic climb, consider the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40011874" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Sa Creueta Pass (780 m) – Coll de Rates loop from Olla Altea&lt;/a&gt;, a difficult 91.7 km route with significant elevation gain.

Q: Are there any shorter, moderate gravel routes available?
A: For a moderate gravel biking experience that is shorter in distance, you can explore routes like the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/23746330" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Quisi Mill – View of the Railway Viaduct loop from Parc Natural del Penyal d'Ifac&lt;/a&gt;. This route covers 24.9 km with a moderate difficulty, offering a good option for those looking for a less strenuous but still engaging ride.

Q: Is public transport or parking available near the gravel trails?
A: While the guide focuses on the trails themselves, Altea is well-connected. For specific routes, starting points often have nearby parking facilities. For public transport access, Altea and surrounding towns like Benidorm (which serves as a starting point for some routes, e.g., Benidorm Intermodal) have bus services. It's advisable to check local transport schedules and parking availability for your chosen starting point.
tips: The trail is absolutely doable! The MTB S0 classification is actually appropriate, and in places it changes to S1, but without any further difficulty. Partly a “cycle path”.

Like a small park with benches to sit on or the opportunity to take a break in a bar. This is the perfect place for this. Polop is located about 10 km from Benidorm in the hinterland of the Costa Blanca and offers a quiet, authentic atmosphere away from mass tourism. A detour through the narrow streets of the town allows you to experience the traditional architecture and the authentic flair of this Spanish community. Polop is perfect for a day trip, far away from the hustle and bustle of the coastal towns.
It is the perfect curve to overtake, stop at its apex and take photos of those who pedal with you on this mythical climb.
Beautiful wavy slopes when coming from the Col de Rates

Very easy to ride: 3-4 meters wide, partly earth, partly fine-grained gravel. Views are particularly to the west. Very quiet, no public transport. But it is NOT a mountain bike route in the true sense of the word. More like a bike with gravel.

It is a picturesque inland town that is very well preserved. It is nestled at the foot of the mountains and among fields of almond trees, olive trees and vineyards. Many of its streets are paved, which gives it an even more unique character.

waypoints.name: A beautiful “tunnel” created by the surrounding vegetation
